title: A microcanonical approach to criticality in the mean-field $φ^4$ model: evidence of intrinsic microcanonical structure before the thermodynamic limit
abstract: Collective critical behavior is often identified with thermodynamic nonanalyticities and divergences emerging only in the infinite-size limit. Here we adopt a complementary viewpoint: criticality is a structural property due to the rearrangement of the interactions among system's constituents that already exists at finite size and becomes singular only asymptotically. We show that the microcanonical entropy derivatives provide a natural finite-$N$ arena where such structure is encoded in intrinsic extremal/inflection morphologies, and that microcanonical inflection-point analysis (MIPA) turns these morphologies into a unique finite-size critical marker and a well-defined critical trajectory. Using the mean-field $φ^4$ model as a stringent benchmark, we reconstruct $β_N(\varepsilon)$ and $γ_N(\varepsilon)$ from microcanonical simulations, validate them against analytic results, and demonstrate that the MIPA trajectory converges to the exact thermodynamic critical point while simultaneously organizing the approach of other observables to their asymptotic behavior. Our results elevate finite-size criticality from a rounded remnant of the thermodynamic limit to a measurable and predictive object in its own right, with direct relevance to modern finite-system platforms and numerical studies.
summary: This paper proposes that criticality is an intrinsic structural property of finite-size systems rather than just an asymptotic phenomenon. By analyzing the morphology of microcanonical entropy derivatives, the authors identify a predictable 'critical trajectory' that converges to the thermodynamic limit. This provides a new way to identify and characterize phase transitions in finite-system platforms.
main_problem: The paper investigates whether phase transitions and criticality can be defined as intrinsic structural properties of finite-size systems rather than just non-analyticities that emerge only in the thermodynamic limit.
main_result: The authors demonstrate that the microcanonical entropy landscape contains well-defined structural markers (inflection points and extrema) at finite N that form a 'critical trajectory' converging to the exact thermodynamic critical point.
method: The study uses Microcanonical Inflection-Point Analysis (MIPA) applied to microcanonical simulations of the mean-field phi^4 model, comparing results against analytical thermodynamic-limit solutions and canonical observables.
model_or_system: A 1D mean-field phi^4 model, which is a paradigmatic system for continuous phase transitions, characterized by a Hamiltonian with a potential term and a long-range coupling strength J.
key_observables: Microcanonical entropy derivatives (inverse temperature beta_N and entropy curvature gamma_N), magnetization, specific heat, and the critical energy marker epsilon_star(N).
important_parameters: System size N, energy density epsilon, temperature T, and the thermodynamic limit N approaching infinity.
main_assumptions: The study assumes that the structural reorganization of the phase-space measure is the fundamental physical driver of phase transitions, independent of the infinite-size limit.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the evolution of entropy derivatives (beta and gamma) and their convergence toward the thermodynamic limit; Figure 2 displays the scaling of the critical trajectory epsilon_star(N) against N^(-1/2); Figure 3 shows canonical signatures like the caloric curve and specific heat peaks.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of finite-size criticality, presents the MIPA framework, applies it to the mean-field phi^4 model as a benchmark, validates the results against analytical solutions, and demonstrates the convergence of the critical trajectory and its ability to organize other thermodynamic observables.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Microcanonical entropy derivatives for the mean-field φ4 model. (a) Inverse temperature βN(ε) reconstructed from microcanonical simulations for several system sizes. (b) Entropy curvature γN(ε) = ∂2ε sN(ε). While βN(ε) varies smoothly across the critical region, the curvature γN(ε) develops a localized extremal structure that becomes progressively sharper with increasing N. This structure reflects a local restructuring of the entropy landscape associated with the onset of critical behavior. The position of this extremal feature defines the finite-size critical marker ε⋆(N) through microcanonical inflection-point analysis (MIPA).

Figure 2 panel (a.1) shows the resulting values of ε⋆(N) ob- tained from the curvature signal γN(ε) for all simulated system sizes, while panel 2(a.2) represents the value of the maxima at the critical energy value, namely, M(N) := γN(ε⋆(N)). Rather than appearing as isolated pseudocritical estimates, these val- ues form a trajectory in the (N−1/2,ε) and (N−1/2,maxε γN) planes, respectively. This trajectory represents the finite- size evolution of the entropy-derivative structure identified in Sec. V. Each point corresponds to the energy at which the microcanonical entropy curvature signals the collective reor- ganization of the phase-space measure for that system size.

FIG. 2. Finite-size critical trajectory extracted from the entropy cur- vature. (a) Energy values ε⋆(N) identified from the extremal struc- ture of γN(ε) for all simulated system sizes. (b) Convergence of ε⋆(N) toward the thermodynamic critical energy εc as a function of N−1/2. The dashed line indicates the exact critical energy. The data reveal a smooth and systematic finite-size flow toward εc, showing that the thermodynamic critical point emerges as the limit of a well- defined family of finite-size entropy structures.

FIG. 3. Canonical finite-size signatures of the phase transition in the mean-field φ4 model.(a) Caloric curve ε(T) for several system sizes. (b) Specific heat CV (T) obtained from canonical simulations. The vertical dashed line indicates the exact critical temperature of the thermody- namic limit. As expected for a continuous transition, the specific heat develops a size-dependent peak that sharpens and shifts with increasing N. At finite system size the thermodynamic singularity is replaced by smooth anomalies whose position defines a pseudocritical temperature. These standard observables clearly signal the approach to criticality, yet they do so through broadened and size-dependent...
